evidence_quotes.quote: dark patterns and other practices on digital platforms that operate to the detriment of consumers.
OEMs often set prices at levels that make it commercially unviable for third-party service providers to develop and offer innovative or competitively priced alternatives.
imposed by OEMs as conditions for access to vehicle-related digital platforms.

paragraphs.text: The Digital Fairness Act aims to ban or at least limit dark patterns and other practices on digital platforms that operate to the detriment of consumers. Its objectives are to strengthen consumer protection against unfair business practices and to improve the enforcement of consumer rights.As AME, we would like to draw attention to another form of dark and hidden pattern that is established through digital meansbefore any user interaction and without user consentwhich can have significant effects on both competition and fair business practices, ultimately harming consumers.This dark pattern lies in the non-transparent and difficult-to-challenge processes governing how competing offers are integratedor excludedfrom digital platforms. Such mechanisms can severely limit consumer choice by restricting the range of available services or products to a small number of alternatives, and in some cases, to a single monopolistic option.This phenomenon is particularly evident in the automotive spare parts and services sector, where increasing digital supervision by Original Equipment Manufacturers (OEMs) extends over the entire service ecosystem surrounding their vehicles. More and more independent providers and developers of automotive parts and services are being pushed out of the market due to technical, procedural, or commercial barriers imposed by OEMs as conditions for access to vehicle-related digital platforms.Of special concern is the pricing of platform access for certain digital services, which should legally comply with the FRAND principle (Fair, Reasonable, and Non-Discriminatory). In practice, OEMs often set prices at levels that make it commercially unviable for third-party service providers to develop and offer innovative or competitively priced alternatives. Consequently, consumers are forced to purchase costly products or services from an oligopoly or even a monopoly, resulting in higher prices and, over time, reduced innovation and competitiveness across the European automotive sector.We therefore strongly welcome the Digital Fairness Acts emphasis on strengthening the enforcement of consumer and competition rules.As a possible solution, we propose the introduction of a mandatory digital reporting requirement for all platform providers. Such reporting should include data on the percentage distribution of service offers and actual sales for specific service types. These indicators would serve as measurable and transparent benchmarks for assessing fair competition and pricing practices within digital markets.
